title: Green tea cold-process soap
subtitle: A pale green bar made with a matcha infusion, olive oil, coconut oil, and castor oil at 5% superfat
excerpt: Matcha powder tints this cold-process bar a soft, muted green that deepens during the gel phase and settles to a sage hue as the bar hardens. The oil blend is 67% olive, 28% coconut, and 5% castor, brewed against a green tea infusion in place of distilled water. Six weeks of curing gives a mild, conditioning bar.
sourceNotes: SAP values used: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied; lye discount 33% of oil weight. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), lye calculation and oil-property chapters. Green tea as a soap liquid: the catechins in brewed tea have no known negative interaction with lye, but the sugars accelerate trace; chill the infusion before use. Matcha as a natural colourant: Bramble Berry natural colourant guide (2023).

recipe.yieldDescription: 8 bars (~125 g each after cure loss)
recipe.batchNotes: Run any change to oil weight through a lye calculator before adjusting the lye figure. Never scale the lye proportionally without recalculating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bars need 48 hours in the mould before unmoulding, then a minimum of 4 weeks on a curing rack before use.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place. Store on an open rack so air circulates around each bar. Discard if bars develop orange oily specks (DOS, dreaded orange spots, a sign of rancid superfat) or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between fat and lye (sodium hydroxide) that produces soap and glycerine. Each oil has a SAP value: the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that oil.
The percentage of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5% superfat, 5% of the oil weight remains as free oil, giving a mild and conditioning bar.
The moment when the soap batter thickens enough that a drizzle on the surface holds its shape briefly before sinking. The signal that the oils and lye solution have emulsified.

A translucent, jelly-like stage the soap batter passes through in the mould as the saponification reaction generates heat. Soap that gels tends to have a denser, smoother surface and deeper colour than soap that does not.

content.text: Green tea replaces distilled water in this cold-process bar, contributing catechins and a very slight tint that the matcha powder deepens to a pale sage green. The sugars in brewed tea accelerate
, so the infusion goes into the freezer before use, and the soap batter is poured at light trace before it thickens further. At 5%

and 33% lye discount, the bar is conditioning without being soft. Cure time is 4 to 6 weeks; the bar mellows and hardens throughout that window. Yield is 8 bars of around 125 g each after cure shrinkage.
Before starting, put on protective gloves and goggles. The lye handling and safety steps come before any mixing.

items.prepNote: 28% of oil weight; solid below 25°C, melt gently
5% of oil weight; improves lather and foam stability
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat
Brewed as a strong green tea infusion (1 tablespoon loose-leaf per 200 ml); chilled to near freezing before use
1 teaspoon; dissolved in 1 tablespoon of oil from the recipe before adding at trace

content.text: Brew a strong green tea using 1 tablespoon of loose-leaf green tea per 200 ml of just-boiled water. Steep for 5 minutes, strain, and leave to cool. Once cool, put the infusion in the freezer until it is slushy but not fully frozen. The near-frozen temperature slows the accelerated trace caused by the tea sugars.

Work in a well-ventilated space. Put on protective gloves and goggles before opening the lye container. Weigh the sodium hydroxide into a small container. Pour the chilled green tea infusion into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the liquid in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add the liquid to the lye. The solution will heat to 70 to 80°C and give off steam. Set the pitcher somewhere safe to cool.

Weigh the olive oil into a stainless-steel or HDPE bowl. Melt the coconut oil gently if solid (microwave in 20-second bursts, or in a saucepan over low heat) and weigh into the same bowl. Add the castor oil. Let the oil blend cool to 38 to 42°C. The lye solution should be at a similar temperature before mixing.
Take 1 tablespoon of the measured oil from the bowl and stir in the matcha powder until smooth. This prevents the powder clumping when it hits the full batter. Set aside.
Pour the lye solution into the oil blend in a slow, steady stream. Use the stick blender in short 5-second bursts, stirring between each, until the batter reaches light
: the consistency of thin custard. Stir in the matcha oil mixture quickly and blend for a further 5 seconds. Work fast; the tea sugars mean this batter moves toward medium trace quickly.

Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould. Tap the mould on the work surface once to settle the batter and release air pockets. Wrap the mould in a towel and leave undisturbed in a warm place for 24 to 48 hours. The heat generated during
will drive the soap through the
, deepening the green. Insulating encourages a full gel and a denser bar.

After 48 hours, turn the mould out. The soap should be firm but not bone-hard. If it is soft or sticky, leave in the mould for another 24 hours. Cut into bars using the wire cutter or a sharp knife. Wear gloves for the first week after cutting; the bar is still caustic until fully cured.

Arrange the bars on a rack with space between each. Leave in a cool, dry room out of direct sunlight. Turn the bars every two weeks so all sides cure evenly. The bars are safe to use after 4 weeks; 6 weeks gives a harder, milder bar. The sage-green colour settles and lightens slightly as the bar dries.

steps.label: Unmould and cut
steps.note: Firm enough to unmould. Still caustic; wear gloves.

steps.label: Turn the bars
steps.note: Flip each bar so the underside cures. The green will have deepened and begun settling.

steps.label: Four-week check
steps.note: Test a sliver on a patch of skin. The bar is mild and ready to use from this point.

steps.label: Cure complete
steps.note: Fully cured bar. Hard, mild, and stable for two years in a cool dark place.

content.text: Store cured bars on an open soap dish or rack that lets air circulate. A closed container traps moisture and shortens shelf life. In a cool dark cupboard, fully cured bars keep two years. Discard any bar that develops orange oily spots (DOS) or smells sharp and paint-like. Both are signs of rancid superfat oil.
